1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 3 Transformer - Used for modeling long dependencies between input sequence elements - Supports parallel processing of sequence - As compared to RNN (e.g. - Allows processing multiple modalities - (e.g., images, videos, text and speech) using similar processing blocks - Typically, pre-trained using pretext tasks on largescale (unlabeled) datasets - Demonstrates excellent scalability to very large networks and huge datasets.
lability to very large networks and huge datasets. - GPT-4 (Generative Pretrained Transformer)

asets.
- GPT-4 (Generative Pretrained Transformer) 1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 4 Natural Language Processing
- BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers),
- GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) v1-4,
- RoBERTa (Robustly Optimized BERT Pre-training)
- T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer)
- Gemini
- LLaMA
- Most recent LLMs and VLMs
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 5

1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 5 Vision Applications - Recognition tasks - e.g., image classification, object detection, and segmentation - Generative modeling, multi-modal tasks - e.g., visual-question answering, visual reasoning, and visual grounding - Video processing - e.g., activity recognition, frame prediction - Low-level vision - e.g., image super-resolution, image enhancement, and colorization) - 3D analysis - e.g., point cloud classification and segmentation)

1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 16

Why?
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 16 Multiple heads
- You break each token (K,V,Q) into multiple tokens
- Along the feature dimension
- For example, 12 in GPT-2
- Each head processes a segment of the embeddings independently
- Capturing different syntactic and semantic relationships.
- This design facilitates parallel learning of diverse linguistic features
- Enhancing the model's representational power.
https://poloclub.github.io/transformer-explainer/

invariant

parallel

take into account

sequence

1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 22 Why positional encodings
- “The teacher said the student is smart.”
- “The student said the teacher is smart.”
- “Only she said he was wrong.”
- “She said only he was wrong.”
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 23

ong.”
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 23 Positional encodings
- The transformer adds a vector to each input embedding.
- These vectors follow a specific pattern that the model
learns
- Learned pattern helps model
- to determine the position of each word, or
- the distance between different words.
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 24

Position Encoding
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 25

Position Encoding
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 26

Position Encoding
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 27

oding
1/27/2026 CAP6412 - Lecture 2 Transformer 27 Position Encoding
- Can also be learned
- Learn like other parameters
- CoPE (Complex Positional Encoding)
- A lightweight method designed to be more flexible
- Enabling positional encoding based on token context
- Rather than just absolute distance.
- RoPE (Rotary Position Embedding)
- To better handle long-context sequences
- By multiplying the attention matrix with a rotation matrix

10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 1

Autoencoder
Lecture 10
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 2

Autoencoder
- Reproduce the input
- Via learning features
- Unsupervised learning
- Efficient way to learn features
- Still need a loss function – implicit supervision
- Supervised learning
- Need labels/annotations
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 3

Autoencoder
- Encoder – decoder
- Encoding
- Key idea
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 4

coding
- Key idea
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 4 Autoencoder
- Compare PCA/SVD
- PCA produce smaller set of vectors
- Approximate the input vectors via linear combination.
- Very efficient for certain applications.
- Autoencoder
- Can learn nonlinear dependencies
- Can use convolutional layers
- Can use transfer learning
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 5

transfer learning
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 5 Autoencoder
- Encoder: h = f(x)
- Compress input into a latent-space
- Usually smaller dimension
- Decoder: r = g(f(x))
- Reconstruct input from the latent space
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 6

Autoencoder
- Encoder: h = f(x)
- Compress input into a latent-space
- Usually smaller dimension
- Decoder: r = g(f(x))

nomaly detection
10/3/2024 CAP5415 - Lecture 10 16 Properties
- Data-specific
- Compress data similar to what they have been trained on
- Lossy
- Outputs will be degraded compared to the original inputs
- Learned automatically from examples
- It is easy to train
- It will perform well on data similar to training samples
- Compare with hand-crafted features
